title: Assisted Ventilation and Its Complications
authors: Donn SM, Stepanovich GE, Attar MA / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Assisted Ventilation and Its Complications?
Mechanical ventilation คือ intervention ที่ทรงพลังที่สุดใน NICU — ช่วยชีวิตทารก 23 wk ที่ไม่มีทางรอดในอดีต แต่ก็เป็น weapon ที่ทำร้ายปอดได้ทุกลมหายใจ. (1) ไม่มี one-size-fits-all: ทุก baby ต้องการ customized strategy ตาม pathophysiology; (2) Mean airway pressure → oxygenation; Amplitude (PIP−PEEP) → ventilation — สองหลักการนี้ควบคุม ventilator ได้ทั้งหมด; (3) VILI = baro + volu + atelectrauma + biotrauma + rheotrauma — ทำลายปอดพร้อมกัน ไม่ทีละอย่าง; (4) Volume-targeted ventilation ลด BPD และ air leak กว่า pressure-limited (Klingenberg Cochrane 2017); (5) CPAP ที่ delivery room + selective LISA คือ standard of care ที่ลด death/BPD อย่างมีนัยสำคัญ. เข้าใจ chapter นี้ = เข้าใจว่าตนเองกำลังทำอะไรกับปอดทารกทุกครั้งที่จับ ventilator.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย physiology ของ CPAP: Laplace's law, FRC maintenance, surfactant conservation, 5 clinical indications
เปรียบเทียบ NCPAP interfaces: binasal prongs vs nasal mask vs RAM cannula — ข้อดีข้อเสีย
อธิบาย HFNC: mechanism, limitation (pressure ไม่แน่นอน), criteria สำหรับใช้แทน CPAP
จำแนก NIPPV modes: nonsync vs sync (SNIPPV), NIV-NAVA (Edi trigger) — ข้อดีและ evidence
อธิบาย INSURE และ LISA: indications, meta-analysis evidence สำหรับ death/BPD reduction
ระบุ absolute vs relative indications สำหรับ assisted ventilation พร้อม 50-50 rule
อธิบาย determinants of oxygenation (mean Paw, PEEP, PIP, TI, rate, flow) vs ventilation (tidal volume × rate)
อธิบาย time constant = RC; ความสำคัญต่อ gas trapping; 3–5 τ rule
จำแนก conventional ventilator modes: IMV, SIMV, A/C, PSV — ข้อดีข้อเสียแต่ละ mode
เปรียบเทียบ pressure-targeted vs volume-targeted (VCV) vs hybrid (VG, PRVC, VAPS) — evidence
อธิบาย HFV: HFJV vs HFOV — rate, TI, exhalation mechanism, clinical indications
interpret pulmonary graphics: P-V loop (hyperinflation, optimal PEEP), F-V loop (resistance), flow waveform (gas trapping)
ระบุ complications: airway injury, VAP, air leaks (PTX, PIE, pneumomediastinum, pneumopericardium), VILI (5 types), BPD, IVH/PVL, ROP
วางแผน weaning strategy: requisites, impediments, SIMV+PSV approach, extubation readiness criteria
1. CPAP และ Noninvasive Ventilation (NIV)
1.1 Physiology ของ CPAP
CPAP = Continuous Distending Pressure (CDP): maintains positive pressure ตลอด expiratory phase ของ spontaneous breathing
Laplace's law: P = 2T/r — CPAP ↑ r (alveolar radius) → ↓ P required ต้าน surface tension → ↓ work of breathing

Mechanisms (รวม 8 อย่าง): (1) prevents alveolar collapse → maintains FRC; (2) stents upper airway → ↓ obstruction; (3) augments stretch receptors → ↓ diaphragm fatigue; (4) conserves surfactant at air-liquid interface; (5) ↓ alveolar edema; (6) ↑ lung compliance; (7) ↓ airway resistance; (8) ↑ tidal volume
CPAP vs PEEP: CPAP = CDP ใน spontaneous breathing infant; PEEP = CDP ใน mechanically ventilated infant — both are types of CDP, NOT ventilation
1.2 NCPAP Interfaces

rows: ข้อเสีย / ข้อพึงระวัง
Most evidence; snug fit; effective pressure delivery; best seal
Nasal trauma (erythema, septum injury, nasal deformity); prongs rarely fit tightly → pressure leak
As effective as prongs; less nasal injury (King BC Neonatology 2019)
↓ nasal trauma vs prongs; useful when facial injury
25–37% pressure loss vs standard interface → need higher set pressure

blocks.text: 1.3 CPAP Methods

Continuous-flow CPAP: Ventilator-derived CPAP (expiratory orifice adjustment); Bubble/underwater CPAP (immersion depth = cm H2O pressure; vibrations may ↑ gas exchange — first described von Reuss 1914)
Variable-flow CPAP: Flow-driven CPAP (Bernoulli + Coanda effects → fluidic flip → ↓ expiratory resistance); Benveniste valve
Clinical indications (5 groups): (1) delivery room resuscitation; (2) RDS management; (3) post-extubation care; (4) apnea treatment; (5) mild upper airway obstruction

CPAP Delivery Room — Evidence and Practice
Initial CPAP pressure: 5–6 cm H2O by convention (expert consensus; little RCT data on optimal). Subramaniam meta-analysis (Cochrane 2016, 3123 VLBW infants): early CPAP vs prophylactic intubation/surfactant → ↓ need for MV, ↓ surfactant use, ↓ BPD at 36 wk, ↓ BPD/death combined. Current practice: CPAP is the optimal first respiratory support modality in the delivery room. Intubation reserved for apnea or inadequate ventilation despite NIV. Animal data: higher initial CPAP (8–12 cm H2O) improves aeration + surfactant response; multicenter RCT ongoing (NCT04111770) comparing 6–8 vs 3–5 cm H2O. Sustained inflation (SI): 20–30 cm H2O × 5–25 s to recruit lung at delivery — mixed results; ไม่ลด death หรือ BPD (meta-analysis Kapadia Pediatrics 2021).
1.4 High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC)
Flow >1 L/min, up to 8 L/min: provides some degree of CPAP via: (1) washout of nasopharyngeal dead space; (2) ↓ inspiratory resistance; (3) degree of CDP
Major limitation: CPAP level NOT measurable in clinical practice — highly variable depending on leak at nose/mouth; cannula tips should not exceed 50% of internal nares diameter

Evidence (Roberts et al NEJM 2016, HFNC vs CPAP primary support >27 wk): more treatment failures with HFNC; meta-analysis 2022 (10 RCTs) confirmed greater HFNC failure but no ↑ reintubation (CPAP rescue permitted); ↓ nasal injury with HFNC
CPAP weaning strategy: gradual wean most effective to keep off CPAP; stepdown to HFNC → longer oxygen therapy (↑ ROP risk if pure O2); extended CPAP (2 wk beyond 32 wk) → ↑ FRC (Lam J Pediatr 2020)
1.5 Noninvasive Ventilation (NIPPV, SNIPPV, NIV-NAVA)
NIPPV terminology (not standardized): includes NIPPV (nonsync), SNIPPV (sync; only available in Europe), SiPAP/BiPAP (bilevel NCPAP), NIV-NAVA (Edi trigger)
NIPPV vs CPAP (meta-analysis): NIPPV superior for reducing extubation failure + reintubation; not superior for apnea events vs CPAP alone (Lemyre Cochrane 2017; Sabsabi Am J Perinatol 2022)

NIV-NAVA — Neurally Adjusted Ventilatory Assist
Mechanism: Edi (electrical activity of diaphragm) measured by catheter at gastroesophageal junction → Edi peak × NAVA level (cm H2O/μV) = PIP delivered. Example: NAVA level 2.0 × Edi peak 10 μV = PIP 20 cm H2O. Advantages over unsync NIPPV: improved infant-ventilator synchrony, ↓ wasted effort, ↓ auto-triggering, ↓ delivered PIP, potentially ↓ cumulative barotrauma. Post-extubation RCT (Makker J Perinatol 2020): NIV-NAVA superior to NIPPV for reintubation within 120h. Currently the ONLY synchronized NIPPV available in the United States. Contraindications: total lack of respiratory drive, esophageal atresia (cannot place Edi catheter). Post-extubation network meta-analysis (Ramaswamy Pediatr Pulmonol 2020): Synchronized NIPPV = best for extubation success; continuous-flow CPAP = highest reintubation risk; HFNC = least nasal trauma + least air leak; S-NIPPV = lowest BPD rate.
1.6 INSURE และ LISA
INSURE (Intubation-SURfactant-Extubation): indicated when spontaneous breathing intact + FiO2 >0.3 without other MV indications; risk factors for failure: ELBW, GA <26 wk, severe RDS
LISA (Less Invasive Surfactant Administration): surfactant via small catheter/feeding tube to spontaneously breathing baby on CPAP — ไม่ต้อง intubation
OPTIMIST-A RCT (Dargaville JAMA 2021): LISA vs sham → ↓ death/BPD in preterm survivors
Other routes under study: via LMA directly into pharynx; via nebulization (practical challenges: poor aerosolization, uneven distribution, inadequate delivery — still investigational)
2. Indications และ General Principles ของ Assisted Ventilation
2.1 Indications

rows: Failure to initiate/sustain spontaneous breathing; persistent bradycardia despite PPV; major airway anomalies (CDH, severe hydrops); sudden respiratory/cardiac collapse

High likelihood of respiratory failure; surfactant administration; impaired gas exchange; worsening apnea unresponsive to CPAP/methylxanthines; need airway patency (MAS, tracheobronchomalacia); control CO2 (PPHN, HIE); drug-induced respiratory depression (MgSO4, anesthetics)

blocks.text: 50-50 Rule: hypoxemia = PaO2 <50 mmHg with FiO2 ≥0.5; hypercapnia = PaCO2 >50 mmHg (some add pH <7.25). Most widely used criterion for objective respiratory failure assessment
2.2 Determinants of Oxygenation vs Ventilation

Two Fundamental Principles of Mechanical Ventilation
1. OXYGENATION ∝ Mean Airway Pressure (mean Paw): mean Paw = area under pressure-time curve. ↑ by: ↑PEEP (1:1 ratio), ↑PIP, ↑TI, ↑rate, ↑flow. PEEP most direct effect (applied throughout cycle). Excessive PEEP: overdistension + ↑ air leak + ↓ cardiac output + ↓ amplitude → CO2 retention. 2. VENTILATION (CO2 removal) = Tidal Volume × Frequency: Tidal volume determined by amplitude (PIP−PEEP). Adjustment in amplitude has more predictable effect on PaCO2 than rate changes. During HFV: CO2 removal ∝ freq × (Vt)² — thus small amplitude changes have large effect. Key implication: to improve oxygenation → ↑ mean Paw (PEEP, TI, PIP); to reduce CO2 → ↑ amplitude or rate; these can be adjusted independently during HFV more than during CMV.
2.3 Time Constant (τ)
Time constant τ = Compliance × Resistance: time required for pressure + volume equilibration
Stiff lungs (↓ compliance, as in RDS): short τ → baby breathes rapidly + shallow. As RDS resolves → ↑ compliance → ↑ τ → baby breathes slower + deeper
3τ → 95% equilibration; 5τ → 99% equilibration
Clinical rule: expiratory time must be ≥3–5τ to avoid gas trapping, inadvertent PEEP, alveolar rupture. Short babies with stiff lungs → short TI proportional to birth weight
3. Classification of Mechanical Ventilators — Modes และ Modalities
3.1 Control Variables (Ventilatory Modalities)
Ventilator controls only ONE variable at a time: time, pressure, or volume
Pressure control: volume fluctuates with compliance; Volume control: pressure fluctuates with compliance — these are
Pressure-targeted: PLV (fixed continuous flow, time/flow-cycled), PCV (variable decelerating flow, early pressurization — advantage in heterogeneous disease), PSV (spontaneous mode only, flow-cycled)
Volume-targeted: VCV (square wave flow, peak pressure/volume at end inspiration = 'back-end loaded'); PLV is 'front-end loaded'. VCV adjusts PIP automatically as compliance changes → auto-wean
3.2 Phase Variables (Ventilatory Modes)

rows: Baby may breathe with/against/between breaths; wide TV variability; sedation/paralysis often needed; AVOID if spontaneous breathing present
Onset of MV synchronized to spontaneous breath within timing window
Spontaneous breaths supported only by PEEP (unless + PSV); synchrony during inspiration only (unless flow-cycled)
Each spontaneous effort above threshold → MV breath; backup rate if no effort
Most support; lowering control rate has no effect if baby breathes above rate; wean by ↓PIP not rate; flow- or time-cycled
Supports spontaneous breaths between SIMV breaths; overcomes imposed work of breathing; PSmax = full TV; PSmin = just enough to overcome resistance; gaining acceptance

blocks.text: 3.3 Trigger Mechanisms
Flow triggering: requires ↓ effort than pressure → less work of breathing → preferred in neonates
Impedance triggering: transthoracic electrical impedance during spontaneous breathing → can achieve total synchrony during both inspiration AND expiration (Visveshwara J Perinatol 1991)
Edi trigger (NAVA): diaphragm electrical activity → triggers, cycles, and controls level of support; true neural synchrony
4. Volume-Targeted Ventilation
4.1 Volume-Controlled Ventilation (VCV)
Principal feature: delivers set tidal volume irrespective of compliance by automatically adjusting PIP — as compliance improves → auto ↓ PIP → built-in weaning
Measured at proximal ETT (patient wye piece): critical — compressible volume loss within circuit (especially with ↓ compliance) causes discrepancy between ventilator TV and airway TV
Flow waveform: square wave — peak pressure + volume at END inspiration ('back-end loaded'); contrast with PLV which is 'front-end loaded'
VCV benefit: it is volutrauma (not barotrauma per se) that causes lung injury — controlling TV controls the actual injurious variable
4.2 Hybrid Volume-Targeted Modes

rows: Dual-loop; uses exhaled Vt of PREVIOUS breath → adjusts working pressure for NEXT breath. Target Vt 4–6 mL/kg; Pinsp set 15–20% above needed pressure. Extubate when sustained Vt ≥ target with PIP <10–12 cm H2O. Limitation: slow catch-up on large compliance swings.
Flow-cycled; test breath at 10 cm H2O above PEEP → calculates needed pressure → next 3 breaths at 75%; adjusts ±3 cm H2O each breath. Adjusts based on 4-breath average.
PSV + VCV hybrid within single breath; starts as PSV → if Vt not achieved → transitions to VCV (fixed flow continues, TI prolonged). Requires pulmonary graphics to optimize.
PSV + PRVC hybrid; patient determines rate and TI; pressure adjusted ±3 cm H2O until target Vt achieved.

blocks.text:
Volume-Targeted vs Pressure-Limited — Cochrane Evidence (Klingenberg 2017)
Meta-analysis of volume vs pressure-targeted ventilation: aggregated ALL forms of volume targeting. Outcomes: ↓ duration of ventilation; significantly ↓ air leaks; fewer severe neuroimaging abnormalities; statistically significant ↓ BPD. Singh J Pediatr 2006 (VCV vs PLV in VLBW): trend toward ↓ BPD; 20-month follow-up → ↓ respiratory symptoms + ↓ treatment needs in VCV group (Singh ADC Fetal Neonatal Ed 2009). Practical starting settings: A/C mode; Vt 4–6 mL/kg (measured at proximal ETT); PEEP 4–6 cm H2O; target pH 7.25–7.4, PaCO2 40–60, PaO2 50–80 mmHg. Wean by ↓Vt; switch to SIMV/PSV when control rate <30 bpm.
5. High-Frequency Ventilation (HFV)
5.1 Principles
Definition: tidal volumes LESS than anatomic dead space delivered at very rapid rates
CO2 removal during HFV: ∝ freq × (Vt)² — small ↑ amplitude → large ↓ PaCO2; CO2 removal at lower amplitudes than CMV → can use higher PEEP with wider safety margin
Gas distribution: airway resistance (not compliance) is major determinant; laminar/parabolic flow → gas penetrates center of airways more deeply → bypasses bronchopleural or TEF fistulas
Advantage: better uncoupling of oxygenation (mean Paw) and ventilation (amplitude) than CMV
5.2 HFJV vs HFOV

rows: High-velocity injector (proximal ETT adapter or triple-lumen ETT)
Air leak (PIE, TEF/RDS); low-pressure strategy for PIE
Refractory hypoxemia; primary or rescue; optimal lung volume strategy
Blood gases + CXR; 2 O2 blenders (both must be adjusted)
Blood gases + CXR + chest wiggle factor; watch cardiac output

blocks.text: HFJV multicenter trial (Keszler J Pediatr 1991): HFJV superior to rapid-rate IMV for PIE in preterm infants — ↓ time to resolution, ↑ survival in VLBW

HFOV as rescue + iNO delivery: HFOV is the best modality to deliver inhaled NO because it recruits lung volume and ↑ V/Q matching. Drawback: cannot monitor baby as closely; watch for choke points (high amplitude → small airway collapse during active exhalation)
HFV vs CMV for BPD (Lampland Clin Perinatol 2007): large pool of data suggesting advantage → but cumulative RCT evidence does NOT show ↓ BPD. Remain cautious.
6. Weaning และ Extubation
6.1 Physiologic Requisites for Weaning
1. Adequate spontaneous drive: observe breathing pattern; measure TV + frequency → calculate minute ventilation (normal 240–360 mL/kg/min)
2. Overcome respiratory system load: elastic + resistive forces; work of breathing = integral of P-V loop area
3. Adequate nutrition: calories to fuel work of breathing; excess non-nitrogen calories → ↑ CO2 production → impedes weaning

Impediments to Weaning — ต้องแก้ก่อน extubate
Infection; Neurologic/neuromuscular dysfunction; Electrolyte imbalance; Metabolic alkalosis (common → ↓ CO2 drive → สาเหตุที่คนลืมบ่อย); Congestive heart failure; Anemia; Sedatives/analgesics; Malnutrition (inadequate calories OR excessive non-nitrogen calories). Most common reason for failure to wean: failure to wean — clinicians hesitate; check these impediments systematically.
6.2 Weaning Strategy
Reduce most harmful parameter first: change one parameter at a time (except HFOV); document responses
A/C weaning: ↓ PIP (keep TV + PaCO2 in range); lowering control rate has no effect if baby breathes above rate; extubate from A/C or switch to SIMV/PSV
SIMV + PSV (analogous to A/C but independent adjustment): start PSmax (full TV from PSV); reduce SIMV rate; reduce PSV until TV 3–4 mL/kg (PSmin); extubate at this level if regular drive

Extubation readiness (10-min ETT CPAP trial): assess stability during endotracheal CPAP; spontaneous TV at/above target; minute ventilation test (Gillespie J Perinatol 2003; Wilson J Perinatol 1998) — spontaneous MV predicts readiness

Post-extubation: NCPAP or NIPPV; prone positioning ↑ gas exchange (↑ chest wall stability + ↓ diaphragm compression); post-extubation stridor → inhaled sympathomimetics or short steroid course; methylxanthines = only pharmacologic agent with sufficient evidence for weaning
7. Monitoring ของ Ventilated Infants
7.1 Gas Exchange Monitoring
ABG (gold standard): must interpret in clinical context (e.g., PaCO2 65 mmHg alarming in day-1 RDS, acceptable in chronic BPD)
SpO2 target: 88–92% in preterm on O2 (SUPPORT trial NRN); SpO2 >92% → risk of hyperoxia → ↑ ROP; TcPO2 >80 mmHg → ↑ ROP; maintain PaO2 60–80 mmHg

SUPPORT trial (Carlo NEJM 2010, 1316 infants 24–28 wk): lower SpO2 range (85–89%) → ↓ ROP but ↑ mortality (small but significant) → optimal saturation target remains unknown; current consensus 91–95% in many centers
TcPCO2: sensor 42°C; 80–90% sensitivity for hyper/hypocapnia; falsely high in severe shock; unreliable if PaCO2 >45 + pH <7.30
Closed-loop O2 control (Europe, not yet FDA approved): device adjusts FiO2 based on SpO2 → ↑ time in target range + ↓ manual interventions (multicenter trial Claure Pediatrics 2011)
Base deficit: healthy term infant: 3–5 mEq/L; 5–10 mEq/L with OK perfusion: observe; >10 mEq/L: assess hypoperfusion → treat underlying cause (NaHCO3 is the LAST resort, not first)
7.2 Pulmonary Graphics

Reading Pulmonary Graphics — Pattern Recognition
P-V Loop (pressure-volume): compliance axis slope (45° = 1.0 mL/cm H2O); more vertical = ↑ compliance; more horizontal = ↓ compliance. Hyperinflation: upper inflection point — above it, ΔV per ΔP ↓ → 'dog-leg' loop → reduce PIP. Optimal PEEP: box-shaped loop → need ↑ PEEP + PIP to normalize. Resistance (bowing around compliance axis): area to left of compliance line = work against resistance; seen in MAS, BPD. F-V Loop (flow-volume): normally oval, symmetrical around abscissa. ↓ PIF + ↓ PEF + smaller loop = ↑ resistance; successful bronchodilator → larger loop (↑ both PIF + PEF). Flow Waveform: gas trapping = expiratory flow fails to return to zero baseline before next breath; ↑ expiratory resistance = ↓ peak expiratory flow + prolonged return to baseline. ETT leak: P-V loop fails to close; volume waveform fails to reach baseline.
TV targets: ventilated breath 4–7 mL/kg; spontaneous breath 3–4 mL/kg = ready for wean/extubation; minute ventilation 240–360 mL/kg/min
8. Complications of Assisted Ventilation
8.1 Airway Complications

Upper airway: mucosal erosion, dental ridge damage (→ abnormal dentition; Moylan J Pediatr 1980), esophageal perforation, vocal cord injury; long-term nasotracheal → nasal septum erosion + deformity; orotracheal → acquired palatal groove, cleft palate

Tracheal complications: mucosal metaplasia, subglottic cysts, tracheal enlargement, tracheobronchomalacia (↑ duration, ↑ intubations, ↑ prematurity), necrotizing tracheobronchitis (HFJV era — inadequate humidification; now rare)
Subglottic stenosis: ↑ with ↑ duration of intubation + ↑ intubations + ↑ prematurity; potentially requires tracheostomy
8.2 Ventilator-Induced Lung Injury (VILI) — 5 Types

VILI — 5 Types ที่ทำงานร่วมกัน (Attar & Donn Semin Neonatol 2002)
1. Barotrauma: excessive pressure → disrupts airway epithelium + alveoli. 2. Volutrauma: overdistension/stretching of lung units → IT IS VOLUME, not pressure per se, that causes lung injury (Dreyfuss & Saumon Intensive Care Med 1992) → tidal volume control more critical than pressure alone. 3. Atelectrauma: cyclic recruitment-derecruitment → repetitive opening/closing of lung units → shear stress at junction of open/closed alveoli. 4. Biotrauma: infection + inflammation + oxidative stress on developing lung → cytokine cascade → systemic spread. 5. Rheotrauma: inappropriate airway flow — too high → turbulence, inadvertent PEEP, hyperinflation; too low → air hunger, ↑ work of breathing. Cumulative effect: ↓ alveolarization → ↓ pulmonary surface area → BPD. Premature lung is more susceptible: surfactant deficiency, immature antioxidant system, immature structure, compliant chest wall.
8.3 Air Leaks

rows: Most common symptomatic air leak; tension = agitation + ↑ distress + hypotension + ↓ BS + contralateral heart shift; transillumination + CXR confirms; needle aspiration then chest tube. Ventilator risk factors: long TI, inverse I:E, high PIP/mean Paw, high flow, patient-ventilator asynchrony

Terminal airway rupture → gas in interstitium → compresses airways + alveoli → ↓ compliance + ↑ resistance + ↑ gas trapping + impedes pulmonary blood flow. CXR: fine linear/radial radiolucencies. Unilateral → decubitus positioning or selective right mainstem intubation (for L-PIE). Generalized → ↓ TI/pressure; ↑ PEEP to stent airways; HFJV (Keszler J Pediatr 1991)
Usually minor; rarely needs drainage; 100% O2 nitrogen washout (anecdotal); signals ↑ risk for more air leaks
Cardiac tamponade emergency; transillumination + CXR (air surrounds heart); pericardiocentesis via subxiphoid → pericardial drain; occurs with high PIP + mean Paw + long TI
Usually visceral rupture/perforation; rarely trans-diaphragmatic from thoracic air leak. Aspirate abdominal gas → FiO2 >0.21 in sample = thoracic source = avoid unnecessary laparotomy

blocks.text: 8.4 VAP
Definition: pneumonia + new persistent focal CXR infiltrates appearing >48h after NICU admission in ventilated infant; incidence 0.3–1.6 cases/1000 ventilator-days (Level 2–3 NICU, United States)
Risk factors: prematurity; steroids, H2 blockers, antacids, PPIs; overcrowding; understaffing; equipment contamination; prolonged/repeated intubation

Diagnosis: deteriorating respiratory status + temperature instability + glucose instability + metabolic acidosis + abdominal distension + ↑ CRP/WBC. Note: tracheal aspirates reflect colonization, NOT diagnostic
Management: broad-spectrum antibacterial ± antifungal; hemodynamic support; maintain MV as needed; iNO/ECMO for refractory hypoxemia in term/late-preterm
8.5 Neurologic Complications

IVH/PVL risk factors from MV: hypercapnia (days 1–4 life → ↑ PV-IVH risk); hypocapnia (PaCO2 <35 mmHg → ↑ cerebral ischemia → PVL, especially rapid ↓ with HFV initiation); patient-ventilator asynchrony → fluctuating cerebral blood flow → IVH; high PIP/PEEP → ↑ intrathoracic pressure → ↓ venous return → ↓ CO
Autoregulation absent in preterm: pressure-passive cerebral circulation → CBF fluctuates with systemic BP → hemorrhage during hypertension, ischemia during hypotension
Procedural threats: ETT suctioning, reintubation → may compromise cerebral perfusion transiently
8.6 ROP
Major risk factors: prematurity + high arterial oxygen content; hyperoxia, hypoxemia, and oxygen fluctuations within normal range all implicated
Target: PaO2 60–80 mmHg; SpO2 88–92% (↓ ROP risk without excess mortality — balance per SUPPORT trial) — optimal saturation range remains unknown
Additional risks: vitamin E deficiency, exchange transfusion, NEC, PDA treatment, other prematurity complications
9. Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia (BPD) — Ventilation Perspective
9.1 Old vs New BPD
Old BPD (Northway 1967): 32–39 wk GA; 1474–3204 g; high airway pressures + high FiO2; CXR: overinflation + cystic emphysema
New BPD: more immature + VLBW; only modest oxygen + ventilatory support; CXR: diffuse haziness + fine lacy pattern; ไม่มี typical RDS; many do not require MV first few days
Definition: chronic respiratory insufficiency + supplemental O2 requirement + abnormal CXR at 36 wk PMA
9.2 Ventilation Strategies to Reduce BPD
Avoid intubation: CPAP + selective surfactant (INSURE/LISA) → ↓ death/BPD vs prophylactic intubation/surfactant

Permissive hypercapnia: rationale: ↓ ventilation → ↓ volutrauma + ↓ MV duration. BUT: failed to show ↓ BPD; difficult in patient-triggered era (baby with intact chemoreceptors ↑ MV to achieve normocapnia)
Volume targeting (4–6 mL/kg): compelling evidence that excess TV causes lung injury → meta-analysis shows ↓ BPD with volume-targeted MV (Peng Arch ADC 2014)
HFV: animal + human data suggest advantage over CMV → but cumulative RCT evidence does NOT show ↓ BPD (Lampland Clin Perinatol 2007; Stark NEJM 2002)
10. NICU Practical — Ventilator Management Bedside
10.1 Initiating Ventilation — Checklist
Mode choice: A/C offers most support for acute phase; SIMV+PSV for weaning phase; VG/PRVC for volume-targeting throughout
Starting settings (preterm): Vt 4–6 mL/kg (measure at patient wye); PEEP 4–6 cm H2O; TI proportional to birth weight + short for stiff lungs; rate normocapnic; FiO2 per SpO2 target
Starting settings (term): Vt 5–8 mL/kg; PEEP 4–5 cm H2O; adjust per blood gas + clinical
Bias (circuit) flow: adequate to achieve PIP within TI; not too high (turbulence, inadvertent PEEP, hyperinflation); not too low (air hunger, asynchrony)

VG setup box (Box 66.7): press VG; set Vt 4–6 mL/kg; set Pinsp 15–20% above working PIP needed; connect infant; verify delivered Vt + PIP; adjust Pinsp to actual PIP; weaning = automatic as compliance improves
10.2 Troubleshooting Ventilated Infants
Worsening oxygenation: ↑ FiO2; then ↑ mean Paw (↑ PEEP first, then TI, then PIP/rate); check for pneumothorax (transillumination) + ETT position + PPHN
↑ PaCO2: ↑ amplitude (↑ PIP or ↑ Vt); or ↑ rate (less predictable); check for gas trapping on graphics

Baby fighting ventilator: switch from IMV → synchronized mode first; assess pain/discomfort; optimize TI (match respiratory time constant); check for metabolic cause; neuromuscular paralysis only as LAST resort for preventing IVH with severe asynchrony
↑ PCO2 rapidly after starting HFV: DANGER — rapid ↓ PaCO2 → cerebral vasoconstriction → PVL; start at conservative amplitude and titrate; check TcPCO2 continuously
10.3 Extubation Readiness Assessment
Spontaneous TV at/above target (3–4 mL/kg); PIP used by VG <10–12 cm H2O with good respiratory drive
10-minute ETT CPAP trial — assess stability; high PPV for extubation success (Kamlin ADC Fetal Neonatal Ed 2008)
Minute ventilation test (Wilson J Perinatol 1998): spontaneous MV >15 mL/kg/min on 1-min ETT CPAP → predictive of success
Post-extubation: NCPAP or SNIPPV/NIV-NAVA; methylxanthine prophylaxis; prone positioning when stable
11. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Mean Paw = O2; Amplitude = CO2: สองหลักการควบคุม ventilator ทุกตัว
ทุกครั้งที่เห็น hypoxemia → คิดถึง mean airway pressure: ↑PEEP (direct 1:1 effect), ↑PIP, ↑TI, ↑rate, ↑flow → ล้วนเพิ่ม mean Paw. ทุกครั้งที่เห็น hypercapnia → คิดถึง amplitude (PIP−PEEP) และ rate. ใน HFV: CO2 ∝ freq × (Vt)² → small ↑ amplitude = large ↓ PaCO2. ข้อระวัง: ↑PEEP เกินไป → overdistension + ↓ cardiac output + ↓ amplitude → paradoxically ↑ CO2. เข้าใจหลักการนี้ก่อนจับ ventilator ทุกครั้ง.

PEARL 2 — VoluTRAUMA ไม่ใช่ BaroTRAUMA: ควบคุม tidal volume ก่อน pressure
Dreyfuss & Saumon Intensive Care Med 1992 พิสูจน์ว่า lung injury เกิดจาก excessive tidal volume (volutrauma) ไม่ใช่ pressure per se. ดังนั้น volume-targeted ventilation (VG/VCV) — ไม่ใช่ pressure-limited — เป็น physiologically correct approach. Clinical implication: เมื่อ compliance ↑ หลัง surfactant → ใน PLV: Vt ↑ โดยที่ PIP คงที่ → volutrauma; ใน VG/VCV: PIP ↓ auto → Vt คงที่. เฝ้าระวัง compliance change หลัง surfactant ทุกครั้งโดยเฉพาะถ้าใช้ PLV.

PEARL 3 — Gas Trapping: อ่าน flow waveform ก่อน baby deteriorates
Gas trapping = expiratory flow waveform fails to return to zero before next breath. สาเหตุ: TE สั้นกว่า 3–5τ; rapid rate; high flow; ↑ airway resistance (MAS, BPD). ผลลัพธ์: inadvertent PEEP → ↑ air leak risk + ↑ mean Paw → hyperinflation + ↓ cardiac output. Detection: pulmonary graphics ตรวจพบก่อน clinical deterioration. Solution: ↑ TE (↓ rate หรือ ↑ TI:TE ratio); ↓ flow; พิจารณา HFJV ถ้า PIE. อ่าน graphics ทุก shift — ไม่ใช่แค่ตอน baby deteriorate.

PEARL 4 — HypoCAPNIA จาก HFV: PVL ที่ป้องกันได้
เมื่อ switch จาก CMV → HFV: CO2 removal เพิ่มขึ้นมากกว่าที่คาดเนื่องจาก CO2 ∝ (Vt)². Rapid ↓ PaCO2 <35 mmHg → cerebral vasoconstriction → ↓ CBF → periventricular leukomalacia. การป้องกัน: (1) เริ่ม amplitude ต่ำกว่าที่คาด; (2) continuous TcPCO2 monitoring หลัง HFV initiation; (3) target PaCO2 40–55 mmHg ใน acute phase; (4) check ABG ใน 30 min แรก. Hypocapnia จาก aggressive ventilation เป็น preventable cause ของ white matter injury.

PEARL 5 — LISA > INSURE > Prophylactic Intubation: hierarchy ของ surfactant delivery
2025 evidence hierarchy: (1) LISA (surfactant via thin catheter ขณะ CPAP โดยไม่ intubate) → ↓ death/BPD มากที่สุด (OPTIMIST-A JAMA 2021; Dargaville); (2) INSURE (intubate-surfactant-extubate) → ↓ death/BPD vs ไม่ให้ surfactant; (3) prophylactic intubation → ↓ benefit, ↑ MV exposure. ดังนั้น goal คือ: CPAP ใน delivery room → ถ้า FiO2 >0.3 พิจารณา LISA → ถ้า LISA ล้มเหลว → INSURE หรือ MV. Nebulized surfactant ยังเป็น investigational (delivery ไม่สม่ำเสมอ).
12. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Subramaniam P et al Cochrane 2016 (CD001243): meta-analysis 3123 VLBW; CPAP from delivery room vs prophylactic intubation/surfactant → ↓ MV, ↓ surfactant use, ↓ BPD@36wk, ↓ BPD/death combined
Dargaville PA et al JAMA 2021;326:2478 (OPTIMIST-A): LISA vs sham in CPAP-supported preterm with RDS → ↓ death/BPD in survivors; established LISA as preferred surfactant strategy
Roberts CT et al NEJM 2016;375:1142: HFNC vs CPAP as primary support for RDS >27 wk → more treatment failures with HFNC → CPAP remains first choice for primary support
Bruet S et al Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed 2022;107:56: systematic review HFNC vs CPAP 10 RCTs → greater treatment failure HFNC but no ↑ reintubation (CPAP rescue); ↓ nasal injury HFNC
Makker K et al J Perinatol 2020;40:1202: NIV-NAVA vs NIPPV post-extubation → NIV-NAVA superior; less reintubation within 120h — supports NIV-NAVA as post-extubation NIV of choice in US

Ramaswamy VV et al Pediatr Pulmonol 2020;55:2924: network meta-analysis post-extubation NIV: Sync NIPPV = best extubation success; continuous-flow CPAP = most reintubation; HFNC = least air leak/trauma; S-NIPPV = lowest BPD
Lam R et al J Pediatr 2020;217:66: extended CPAP 2 weeks beyond clinical readiness at ~32 wk → significantly ↑ FRC; supports continuation of CPAP through 32–34 wk PMA for lung growth
Dreyfuss D, Saumon G Intensive Care Med 1992;18:139: barotrauma is volutrauma — excessive volume (not pressure per se) causes lung injury; foundational for volume-targeted ventilation rationale

Klingenberg C et al Cochrane 2017 (CD003666): volume- vs pressure-targeted MV in neonates: ↓ ventilation duration, ↓ air leaks, ↓ severe neuroimaging abnormalities, ↓ BPD — strongest RCT evidence for volume targeting
Singh J et al J Pediatr 2006;149:308: VCV vs PLV in VLBW → trend ↓ BPD; 20-month follow-up: ↓ respiratory symptoms + ↓ treatment need in VCV group (Singh ADC 2009;94:F360)
Attar MA, Donn SM Semin Neonatol 2002;7:353: pulmonary injury sequence leading to VILI: barotrauma, volutrauma, atelectrauma, biotrauma, rheotrauma — definitive classification still used today
Keszler M et al J Pediatr 1991;119:85: multicenter RCT HFJV vs rapid-rate IMV in newborns with PIE → HFJV superior: ↓ time to resolution, ↑ survival in VLBW — established HFJV as standard for PIE
SUPPORT Study Group (Carlo WA) NEJM 2010;362:1959: lower SpO2 target (85–89%) in 24–28 wk preterm → ↓ ROP but ↑ mortality; optimal saturation range still unknown
Claure N et al Pediatrics 2011;127:e76: multicenter crossover study of automated closed-loop O2 control → ↑ time in SpO2 target range + ↓ manual nurse interventions

Lampland AL, Mammel MC Clin Perinatol 2007;34:129: role of HFV in neonates: evidence-based review — HFV shows no consistent reduction in BPD despite theoretical advantages; evidence base remains limited
Vliegenthart RJS et al Neonatology 2017;112:172: Netherlands single-center: restrictive MV policy + liberal methylxanthines → ↓ death/neurodevelopmental impairment at 24 months
Wilson BJ et al J Perinatol 1998;18:436: spontaneous minute ventilation >15 mL/kg/min during 1-min ETT CPAP → high PPV for extubation success

Peng W et al Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed 2014;99:F158: systematic review volume-targeted vs pressure-limited: volume targeting more suitable for preterm infants — consistent with Klingenberg Cochrane
13. Self-Check Q&A
Q: อธิบาย CPAP mechanism และ clinical indications ทั้ง 5 กลุ่ม

ตอบ: Mechanism: Laplace's law: CPAP ↑ alveolar radius → ↓ closing pressure → maintains FRC, ↓ work of breathing, conserves surfactant, stents upper airway, ↑ diaphragm tone. 5 Indications: (1) delivery room resuscitation; (2) RDS management; (3) post-extubation support; (4) apnea treatment; (5) mild upper airway obstruction. Starting pressure: 5–6 cm H2O by convention (expert consensus). Early CPAP ↓ need for MV + surfactant + ↓ BPD (Subramaniam Cochrane 2016).
Q: Mean airway pressure กับ oxygenation — อะไร contribute บ้าง และ excess ทำให้เกิดอะไร?

ตอบ: 5 determinants: PEEP (1:1 direct), PIP (× TI), TI (duration of positive pressure), rate (↑ mean Paw at ↑ rate), flow (↑ volume per TI). PEEP most important: applied throughout cycle; 1 cm H2O PEEP = 1 cm H2O mean Paw. Excessive PEEP: overdistension → ↑ air leak; ↓ venous return → ↓ cardiac output; ↓ amplitude → CO2 retention. Balance: optimal PEEP maintains FRC without overdistension — P-V loop shape guides this (box-shape = ↑ PEEP; hyperinflation upper inflection point = ↓ PIP).
Q: Time constant คืออะไร เกี่ยวข้องกับ TI/TE setting อย่างไร?

ตอบ: τ = Compliance × Resistance: time for pressure + volume equilibration. ↓ compliance (RDS) → short τ → baby breathes fast + shallow; as RDS resolves → ↑ τ → slower + deeper. Clinical rule: TE must ≥ 3τ (95% equilibration) to avoid gas trapping; ideally 5τ (99%). Practical: small stiff-lung preterm → short TI (proportional to BW); monitor flow waveform for expiratory flow failing to reach zero = gas trapping. Rapid-rate ventilation (>60 bpm) may cause inadvertent PEEP if TE < 3τ.
Q: เปรียบเทียบ IMV, SIMV, A/C, PSV — indications + weaning applications

ตอบ: IMV: no synchrony → wide TV variability → AVOID ถ้ามี spontaneous breathing; use ONLY if complete apnea. SIMV: synchronized onset but spontaneous breaths between supported by PEEP only (unless + PSV); wean by ↓ rate. A/C: every spontaneous effort → mechanical breath + backup rate; most support; wean by ↓ PIP (NOT rate since ↓ control rate ineffective if baby breathes above it). PSV: spontaneous mode only; supports between SIMV breaths; PSmax = full TV; PSmin = overcome imposed work; best weaning strategy combined with SIMV; extubate from PSmin (TV 3–5 mL/kg).
Q: Volume Guarantee ventilation: ทำงานอย่างไร เริ่มตั้งค่าอย่างไร สัญญาณว่าพร้อม extubate?

ตอบ: Mechanism: dual-loop; uses exhaled Vt of PREVIOUS breath → auto-adjusts working pressure for NEXT breath to maintain target Vt. Starting: SIPPV (= A/C) mode; trigger sensitivity 1 (most sensitive); Vt target 4–6 mL/kg; Pinsp set 15–20% above actual working PIP needed (buffer for compliance drop). Weaning is automatic: as lung improves → PIP auto ↓ to maintain same Vt. Extubation criteria: sustained Vt ≥ target at/above target with PIP <10–12 cm H2O + good sustained respiratory effort.
Q: HFJV vs HFOV — จำแนก rate, exhalation, indications

ตอบ: HFJV: 360–450 bpm; TI ~20 ms; passive exhalation; uses tandem CMV (for PEEP + sigh breaths); gas from high-velocity injector. Use: PIE (low-pressure strategy), TEF+RDS (bypasses fistula), air leaks. HFOV: 480–900 bpm (8–15 Hz); TI 10–20 ms; active exhalation (piston — negative pressure); NO tandem CMV. Use: refractory hypoxemia, optimal lung volume, iNO delivery (best match for V/Q). Watch: chest wiggle factor; avoid high-amplitude choke points; cannot monitor baby closely.
Q: VILI 5 types: ชื่อ mechanism และ how to prevent

ตอบ: Barotrauma: excess pressure → epithelial disruption → ↓ PIP. Volutrauma: overdistension = THE main injury mechanism → ↓ Vt with volume-targeted MV (4–6 mL/kg). Atelectrauma: cyclic opening/closing → optimize PEEP to prevent derecruitment. Biotrauma: infection/inflammation/oxidative stress → aggressive infection treatment, ↓ O2 exposure. Rheotrauma: inappropriate flow → set flow adequate but not excessive; watch P-V loop for hyperinflation and flow waveform for turbulence signs.
Q: Tension pneumothorax: clinical signs, diagnosis, management

ตอบ: Signs: agitation + ↑ respiratory distress + hypotension + decreased/absent BS on affected side + contralateral shift of heart sounds + worsening ABG (respiratory/mixed acidosis + hypoxemia). Diagnosis: transillumination (↑ light transmission on involved side) = bedside; CXR confirmatory. Management: emergency; needle aspiration (thoracentesis) as temporizing measure → chest tube (thoracostomy) for resolution. Prevention: synchronized ventilation (↓ asynchrony); ↓ PIP/mean Paw/TI; avoid inverse I:E; optimize flow; volume-targeted ventilation (Klingenberg Cochrane 2017: ↓ air leaks with volume targeting).
Q: SpO2 target ใน preterm on O2 — SUPPORT trial findings และ current implications

ตอบ: SUPPORT trial (Carlo NEJM 2010, 1316 infants 24–28 wk): randomized to lower (85–89%) vs higher (91–95%) SpO2 range. Lower range: ↓ ROP (significant) BUT ↓ ↑ mortality (small but significant). Current practice: consensus SpO2 91–95% in many centers balancing both risks; maintain PaO2 60–80 mmHg; TcPO2 >80 mmHg → ↑ ROP; SpO2 >92% → risk of hyperoxia + ↑ ROP. Optimal saturation range REMAINS UNKNOWN.
Q: สัญญาณว่า baby พร้อม weaning + ขั้นตอน extubation readiness assessment

ตอบ: Signs for weaning: ↑ spontaneous breathing proportion; ↑ assumption of work of breathing; improved gas exchange + pulmonary mechanics. Impediments to address: infection, metabolic alkalosis, anemia, sedatives, malnutrition, electrolyte imbalance, CHF. Extubation readiness: spontaneous TV ≥ 3–4 mL/kg; VG PIP <10–12 cm H2O; 10-min ETT CPAP trial = gold standard observation; spontaneous MV >15 mL/kg/min = high PPV (Wilson 1998). Post-extubation: NIV-NAVA or NIPPV + methylxanthine.
Q: อธิบาย LISA และ INSURE — indications, evidence, ทำไม LISA ดีกว่า?

ตอบ: INSURE: intubate → give surfactant → extubate to CPAP. Indicated: spontaneous breathing intact, FiO2 >0.3 without other MV indications. LISA: thin catheter/feeding tube → surfactant given while baby breathes spontaneously on CPAP — NO intubation, NO PPV during administration. Why LISA superior: avoids ET tube placement completely → ↓ airway trauma; maintains spontaneous breathing; minimizes exposure to positive pressure. Evidence (OPTIMIST-A, JAMA 2021): LISA → ↓ death/BPD in survivors. Multiple meta-analyses: LISA + CPAP → ↓ BPD vs CPAP alone.

Day 164 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.67
บทต่อไป: Chapter 67 — Spectrum of Neonatal Respiratory Disorders. เตรียมตัว: transient tachypnea of the newborn (TTN): mechanism (fluid clearance delay), CXR pattern, management; meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS): pathophysiology (ball-valve obstruction + chemical pneumonitis + PPHN), management algorithm, role of HFJV; persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN): causes, iNO dosing, ECMO criteria; congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH): lung hypoplasia, gentle ventilation approach; pulmonary hypoplasia; other respiratory disorders.
